name: Auge Valley Chicken Stew
recipeIngredient: 1 lb white mushrooms, washed and minced
2 onions - one sliced, one studded with cloves
Salt and fresh ground black pepper
1 cup crème fraîche or whipping cream
recipeInstructions:
For the apples I used Granny Smith's. For the crème fraîche or whipping cream, I used the whipping cream.
For the Calvados – which would have been too expensive to use, even if I had located it over here – I substituted an apple-flavored liqueur and added a little brandy (Calvados is a dry apple brandy from Calvados in Northern France – very good, but I couldn't find it locally).
In a cast iron casserole, lightly brown the chicken in half the butter and 2 tablespoons oil. When all the pieces are nice and golden, add the carrots, shallots, and sliced onion and cook for a few minutes. Pour the white wine and 1 cup of water over the chicken, then add the clove-studded onion and garlic. Add some thyme leaves and bay leaves and season with salt and pepper. Cover and simmer over low heat for 40 minutes.
While this is cooking for 40 minutes, peel and mince the mushrooms. Remove the chicken from the heat, arrange the pieces in an ovenproof serving dish, and keep warm. Pass the vegetables through a sieve. Set the cooked vegetables aside and keep warm.
Meanwhile, melt the remaining butter in a skillet with the oil. Add the apples and cook until lightly brown on all sides. Remove the apples and arrange them around the chicken. Keep the dish warm in the oven.
Saute the mushrooms until they have released all their juices. Set aside to add to the sauce.
To make the sauce, pour the strained cooking liquid into a saucepan and set over medium heat. With a whisk, beat in the crème fraîche or whipping cream, Calvados (or apple-flavored liqueur), and egg yolks in that order. Simmer gently until it has thickened to a light cream. Stir in the mushrooms and vegetables, discarding the clove-studded onion. Adjust seasoning if needed.
Remove the dish from the oven. Spoon some of the sauce over the chicken and pour the remaining sauce into a serving boat and serve.
